questions.prompt: What are Algolia's ranking criteria and how does tie-breaking work?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "What are Algolia's ranking criteria and how does tie-breaking work?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia ranks search results using a sequential, tie-breaking algorithm that evaluates records against a series of ranking criteria. If records are tied after one criterion, the next is used to determine the order, continuing until all ties are broken.

### Ranking Criteria

Algolia's ranking formula is built on a set of default criteria that prioritize textual relevance first, followed by business-related metrics ([details](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/relevance-overview/in-depth/ranking-criteria/)). The criteria are applied in the following order:

1.  **Typos**: Sorts results based on the number of typos. Records with fewer or no typos rank higher.
2.  **Geolocation**: Ranks results by distance from a specified geographical point.
3.  **Words**: Prioritizes results that contain all query words over those with only some.
4.  **Filters**: Ranks results based on filter matching, which is also used for features like boosting and burying.
5.  **Proximity**: Measures the distance between matched words in an attribute. Records with words closer together rank higher.
6.  **Attribute**: Prioritizes results where matches are found in more important attributes (e.g., a match in `title` is better than in `description`).
7.  **Exact**: A tie-breaking criterion that prioritizes records with more exact word matches.
8.  **Custom Ranking**: Allows you to add your own business metrics to the ranking formula, such as popularity, number of sales, or ratings.

This sequence ensures that the most textually relevant results appear first, after which business goals can influence the final ranking.

### How Tie-Breaking Works

When two or more records are considered equal after applying a ranking criterion, Algolia uses the next criterion in the list to break the tie. This process continues sequentially through all the configured criteria ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used-)).

If records are still tied after all default and custom ranking criteria have been applied, Algolia uses the record's internal **`objectID`** as the final tie-breaker to ensure a consistent and stable sort order ([more info](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean-)). You can influence this by adding more specific custom ranking attributes to your configuration to minimize ties.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why is the 'boost and bury' feature not applied?
The boost and bury features from Rules are based on the optionalFilters parameter. The are two main reasons you may not see items being boosted or buried when you apply a Rule to do so.
These apply to both Rules and Recommend Rules.
1. Ranking and Sorting Configuration
Optional filters are evaluated at the "filters" ranking criterion within the default ranking criteria. 
Because Algolia uses the tie-breaking algorithm, the filters criterion affects the ranking only when precedence criteria do not break the tie.
So for example, if there is a sort-by attribute that is ranked higher than the filters ranking criterion in the Ranking and sorting configuration, boost and bury features will not take effect because the sort by attribute has precedence over any other ranking criterion:

 
In this case it is advised to only add a sort-by attribute to replica indices. Otherwise, lowering the custom ranking criteria below the standard default ranking criteria would enable the proper functioning of boost and bury features.
 
2. Declaring your attribute for faceting
To use an attribute in a Rule that boosts or buries items, you must first declare it as an attributesForFaceting. You can do this either via the:

API: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting/

Dashboard: https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/refine-results/faceting/how-to/declaring-attributes-for-faceting-with-dashboard/
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9821011449233-Why-is-the-boost-and-bury-feature-not-applied

sources.text:
The eight ranking criteria
Discover the eight tie-breaking criteria that Algolia uses to rank your results.
sources.label: /doc/guides/managing-results/relevance-overview/in-depth/ranking-criteria

sources.text:
Why is my search being sorted by geo and not taking into account other ranking criteria?
When ranking hits, geo distances are grouped into ranges of aroundPrecision size. All hits within the same range are considered equal with respect to the geo ranking parameter. For example, if you set aroundPrecision to 100, any two objects lying in the range [0, 99] from the searched location will be considered equal; same for [100, 199], [200, 299], etc. This will allow items that are relatively close to be ranked the same in the "geo" criterion and let the textual criteria do the tie breaking.
In your current setting, aroundPrecision could be too small a number meaning that unless you move geo to the bottom, everything is effectively getting sorted by how far away it is on a meter by meter basis.  In general, we do not advise changing the default ranking formula so it would be preferable to change the aroundPrecision.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4406216902929-Why-is-my-search-being-sorted-by-geo-and-not-taking-into-account-other-ranking-criteria

sources.text:
How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?
When debugging relevancy issues, the Algolia dashboard provides several visual tools to help you understand why records are ranked the way they are for a given query. This guide walks through how to use the Ranking Criteria panel, and explains each ranking criterion you may encounter.Using the Ranking Criteria panelIn the Browse tab on the Algolia dashboard, each record in the results list includes a small medal icon at the bottom-right corner.When you hover over this icon, a panel appears showing the ranking criteria and their values for that record.This panel explains exactly why a record is ranked in its current position relative to others, by displaying how it performed for each ranking criterion:The criteria are shown in the same order as your Ranking and Sorting configuration, which is important: Algolia ranks results using a tie-breaking algorithm that evaluates records sequentially across ranking criteria, stopping as soon as one criterion produces a difference between records. You can change the order of the default criteria, but you shouldn’t. The out-of-the-box ranking order is best for the majority of use cases. Below is a breakdown of what each Ranking criterion means and how to interpret its value in the ranking panel:
Number of typos: Typos required to match the query
Geolocation: Distance from the search location
Matching words: Number of query words matched
Matching filters: Number of filters matched
Proximity distance: Distance between matched words
Best matching attribute: Attribute matched, by priority
Position: Position of matched words in the attribute
Exact word count: Number of exact (no-typo) matches
Custom ranking: Custom ranking value defined by your business metrics (e.g., popularity, ratings)
The ranking criteria panel helps you clearly understand why a record ranks where it does and pinpoint the source of any relevancy issues.Query Alternatives viewCombined with the Ranking Criteria panel, using the Query Alternatives view, gives you a more complete picture of how Algolia interprets queries and ranks results. If you toggle Query Alternatives on the dashboard, you can see how Algolia interprets the query you typed:For a detailed walkthrough, see How to use the Query Alternatives view in the Algolia Dashboard.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard

sources.text:
What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?
The "This result is ranked based on objectID" message indicates that none of your ranking criteria differentiates those results in terms of relevancy.
Algolia uses a tie-breaking algorithm for ranking search results. By default, it works as follows (as described in this document):

It orders all matching records according to the first criterion (number of typos, so exact matches rank first).
For all tied records, it orders them according to the second criterion (geolocation - if applicable).
If there are still tied records (with the same geolocation), it orders them according to the third criterion (filters) and so on, until each record in the search results has a distinct ranking position.
If, after going through the first seven criteria, there are still tied records, Algolia uses each of your custom ranking attributes to break the tie.

After going through all steps and if the custom ranking does not break the tie, Algolia ranks the items based on their objectID, which is the case that this message indicates.
 
If you'd like to avoid such situation and make your ranking criteria meaningful, we recommend reviewing your ranking strategy and consider adding or modifying custom rankings that can break the tie of such items.
You can also find the detail of the default ranking criterion in this document.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean

sources.text:
Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?
Algolia finds the best matches and ranks the search results with its default ranking strategy, using a tie-breaking algorithm. First, it orders ALL matching records using the first criterion (typo by default). For all tie matching records it orders them using the second criterion (geo by default) and if there are still tied matching records, then it orders them according to the third criterion (filters by default), and so on. After going through 8 ranking criteria, it uses any Custom Ranking to break the tie - the engine will go on until there’s no more ties to break.

You can hover your mouse over the little badge shaped icon in the bottom right of each record to check which tie breaker ranked the record in that specific position.
You can increase the importance of your custom ranking criteria by expanding the default ranking criteria and moving your custom criteria up the order. Be extremely careful when doing this - the default order is considered optimal in the majority of cases.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used

answer.answer:
Algolia ranks search results using a tie-breaking algorithm that sequentially applies a set of ranking criteria. If any two results are equal according to one criterion, the engine moves to the next criterion in the list to break the tie, continuing until each result has a distinct rank.

### The Ranking Criteria

Algolia's engine evaluates results using a default order of criteria that is optimal for most use cases [Why is my custom ranking attribute not being used?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/10210296687761-Why-is-my-custom-ranking-attribute-not-being-used). This sequence is designed to balance textual relevance with business-specific metrics [The eight ranking criteria](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/relevance-overview/in-depth/ranking-criteria/).

The criteria are applied in this order:
1.  **Number of typos:** Sorts results based on how many typos are in the query.
2.  **Geolocation:** Ranks results by distance from a specified location.
3.  **Matching words:** Prioritizes results with the most matching query words.
4.  **Matching filters:** Ranks based on the number of filters matched.
5.  **Proximity distance:** Considers the distance between matched words in the results.
6.  **Best matching attribute:** Prioritizes results where the match is in a more important attribute.
7.  **Position:** Ranks based on where the matched words appear in the attribute (earlier is better).
8.  **Exact word count:** Considers the number of words that match exactly (without any typos).
9.  **Custom ranking:** Uses your business metrics (like popularity or rating) to rank results.

You can see exactly how these criteria apply to a specific record by using the ranking criteria panel in the Algolia dashboard [How can I troubleshoot relevancy using Algolia dashboard?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7964661896849-How-can-I-troubleshoot-relevancy-using-Algolia-dashboard).

### How Tie-Breaking Works

The tie-breaking algorithm is the core of Algolia's ranking process. Here’s how it works:

1.  **Sequential Evaluation:** The engine first orders all matching records according to the first criterion (typos).
2.  **Breaking Ties:** If multiple records are tied (e.g., they all have zero typos), the engine uses the second criterion (geolocation) to re-order only that group of tied records.
3.  **Continuation:** This process continues down the list of criteria until every record has a unique position [What does "This result is ranked based on objectID" mean?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/13998838034321-What-does-This-result-is-ranked-based-on-objectID-mean).
4.  **Custom Ranking:** After evaluating the built-in criteria, the engine uses your defined custom ranking attributes to break any remaining ties.
5.  **Final Tie-Breaker:** If, after applying all built-in and custom criteria, some results are still tied, the engine will rank them based on their `objectID` as a final tie-breaker.

Would you like to explore how to set up and configure custom ranking to better align the results with your specific business goals?

questions.prompt: How does the distinct setting deduplicate results?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How does the distinct setting deduplicate results?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

The **distinct** setting in Algolia deduplicates results by grouping items that share a common attribute and only showing the most relevant item from each group. This is useful when you have multiple records representing variations of the same product, like different sizes or colors of a t-shirt, and you only want to show the product once in the search results.

### How It Works

To deduplicate results, you need to configure two parameters:

1.  **`distinct`**: This setting, when enabled, activates the deduplication feature. It identifies all records that have the same value for the `attributeForDistinct` and, from that group, returns only the most relevant result. For example, if a product has five variants (e.g., different sizes), only the single best-matching variant will appear in the results ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results)).

2.  **`attributeForDistinct`**: This is the attribute that Algolia uses to group similar items. You must specify an attribute that uniquely identifies a group of items. For example, in an ecommerce store, you might use `product_id` as the `attributeForDistinct`. All records (variants) sharing the same `product_id` will be considered duplicates.

### Example Use Case

Imagine an online store that sells t-shirts. Each size of a particular t-shirt is a separate record in the Algolia index. Without deduplication, a search for "t-shirt" might return five identical-looking t-shirts in a row, one for each size (S, M, L, XL, XXL).

By setting `distinct` to `true` and `attributeForDistinct` to the t-shirt's model number or product ID, the search results will only show one entry for that t-shirt, representing the most relevant variant ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct-)). This creates a cleaner and more user-friendly search experience.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
[Shopify] Can I display all variant facet values while deduplicating search results?
When you enable “Show products instead of variants” in the Algolia Shopify app, it activates the distinct feature on the id attribute on your Algolia index.The id  attribute represents the “product Id” attribute in your Shopify store, so the distinct feature will use this attribute to limit the number of returned records that contain the same value in that attribute. If your 'Product A' has 5 different sizes, your search will return only the single most relevant record; the other variants that share the same id ("product Id") will not be displayed. Facets will return the facet values that appear in the search results, so if the search results are deduplicated by the distinct parameter, your facets will only display the facet values of the returned variant. While this can be accomplished in non-Shopify implementations of Algolia by setting the facet filter as a non-afterDistinct faceting attribute (as described in this documentation), there is currently no custom hook to modify the default distinct behavior as it relates to faceting in the Algolia Shopify app.  Although we do not have an ETA at this time, our product team have added this functionality to the roadmap for a future release.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results

sources.text:
Why don't my facet counts match the number of hits for attributes set to 'after distinct'?
You may notice that when reviewing your index in the Algolia Dashboard, or on your InstantSearch frontend, that the number of hits returned by a query don't tally with the counts next to some of your facets.If this is the case, check whether the facet showing the incorrect count is set to 'after distinct' as an attribute for faceting.Setting 'after distinct' on an attribute for faceting means that facet counts will be calculated after distinct has been applied. Distinct is a method used to Group Results and is often employed in cases where you want to combine records into a single result, rather than displaying each variant as a separate hit. For example, you might have 5 records for the same t-shirt, one for each size the t-shirt is available in. The issue occurs when combining this with disjunctive faceting. Disjunctive faceting allows for the selection of multiple values within the same facet and to filter on them independently (e.g. size is Medium OR Large). It's the opposite of conjunctive faceting, where the values are treated as dependent (e.g. Medium AND Large).To allow for disjunctive faceting, InstantSearch makes two requests; one to retrieve the hits and one to retrieve the facets. The request to retrieve the facets, counts the deduplicated hits before they have been filtered, and this is what can lead to inconsistencies between the hits and the facet counts.This is a limitation of InstantSearch and the only way to ensure consistent counts under these circumstances is to use conjunctive faceting instead. This can be enabled within your InstantSearch refinementList widget by changing the operator parameter value to 'AND'. There's no way to do this within the Algolia Dashboard. There are several scenarios that could lead to incorrect or inconsistent facet counts. If this article doesn't relate to your issue, please have a look at the below article that describes other issues related to this topic: Facet and hit count issues checklist
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

answer.answer:
The `distinct` feature deduplicates results by grouping records that share the same value for a specific attribute, and then returning only the single most relevant record from each group.

### How it works

The `distinct` setting is a method for grouping results, often used when you want to combine multiple records—like product variants—into a single result.

For example, if you have five records for the same t-shirt in different sizes, `distinct` allows you to show only one of them in the search results instead of all five. The other variants that share the same identifier will not be displayed. This is a common setting in the [Algolia Shopify app](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/26087009806865--Shopify-Can-I-display-all-variant-facet-values-while-deduplicating-search-results-) when you enable "Show products instead of variants."

### Facet counts

It's important to note how `distinct` can affect facet counts. If you set an attribute for faceting to "after distinct," the facet counts will be calculated *after* the deduplication has been applied. However, this can sometimes lead to inconsistencies between the displayed hits and the facet counts when used with disjunctive faceting, which is a known limitation of InstantSearch, as noted in this [support article](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct-).

Are you planning to use this to group product variants, or do you have another use case in mind?

For example, you might have 5 records for the same t-shirt, one for each size the t-shirt is available in. The issue occurs when combining this with disjunctive faceting. Disjunctive faceting allows for the selection of multiple values within the same facet and to filter on them independently (e.g. size is Medium OR Large). It's the opposite of conjunctive faceting, where the values are treated as dependent (e.g. Medium AND Large).To allow for disjunctive faceting, InstantSearch makes two requests; one to retrieve the hits and one to retrieve the facets. The request to retrieve the facets, counts the deduplicated hits before they have been filtered, and this is what can lead to inconsistencies between the hits and the facet counts.This is a limitation of InstantSearch and the only way to ensure consistent counts under these circumstances is to use conjunctive faceting instead. This can be enabled within your InstantSearch refinementList widget by changing the operator parameter value to 'AND'. There's no way to do this within the Algolia Dashboard. There are several scenarios that could lead to incorrect or inconsistent facet counts. If this article doesn't relate to your issue, please have a look at the below article that describes other issues related to this topic: Facet and hit count issues checklist
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27364752291857-Why-don-t-my-facet-counts-match-the-number-of-hits-for-attributes-set-to-after-distinct

questions.prompt: How does personalization re-ranking affect result order?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How does personalization re-ranking affect result order?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Personalization re-ranks search results by adjusting their order based on an individual user's calculated preferences and affinities. This means that instead of seeing a single, static order for results, each user sees a unique order tailored to their past interactions.

### The Ranking Process

Algolia's search engine applies several relevance strategies in a specific sequence to determine the final order of results. Personalization is one of these steps, influencing the ranking after the initial relevance calculation but before other business rules are applied ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38449520711057-Why-are-the-search-results-in-Algolia-different-from-other-search-engines-extensions-and-integrations)).

According to Algolia's process, the relevance strategies are applied in the following order:

1.  **Textual ranking criteria**: The initial matching of the query to the data.
2.  **Business relevance (custom ranking)**: Ranking based on business metrics like "number of sales" or "popularity".
3.  **Personalization**: Re-ranking the results based on the current user's affinity profile. This is the step where the order is adjusted for each individual.
4.  **Boosting and burying with Rules**: Applying rules to promote or demote certain categories.
5.  **Dynamic Re-Ranking**: A feature that can further adjust the order based on specific criteria.
6.  **Pinning and hiding items with Rules**: The final step, where specific items are manually pinned to the top or hidden from results.

Because **Personalization** is applied third in this sequence, it takes the results that have already been ranked for textual and business relevance and then shuffles them to better match the specific user's tastes before other strategies like Dynamic Re-Ranking or manual pinning are considered ([source](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/33835440585361-Why-are-my-products-still-pinned-when-they-go-out-of-stock-)).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why are the search results in Algolia different from other search engines, extensions, and integrations?
If you're switching to Algolia after previously using a search engine that's built-in to your website or integrated into your website by using a third-party extension, then you might wonder why the ordering of your results may be different after adding your records and configuring the index. There are a few reasons why the order of the search results may be different.We use a different relevance criteriaTo order the search results, we use a ranking criteria to define relevance. For our algorithm, the search results rank based on the following criteria:
Typo
Geo
Words
Filters
Proximity
Attribute
Exact
Custom
Attribute and proximity combinations
You can find out more about what this criteria means in our documentation: Ranking Critiera. Searchable Attributes may not include every attribute you'd likeThe Searchable Attributes list is for adding attributes you'd like the search engine to search through while retrieving results. Please check that list to ensure that all preferred attributes are included. Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, and Personalization could be appliedBeyond applying the relevance criteria while ranking results, other factors could be at play due to an index's configuration. If you're using Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, or Personalization, then your search results' ranking could be affected. You can find out more about how these features are applied to ranking here: Effect on Relevance. While we can't comment on how other search engines and extensions rank their results, we hope this article gives you information regarding how Algolia ranks your search results.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38449520711057-Why-are-the-search-results-in-Algolia-different-from-other-search-engines-extensions-and-integrations

sources.text:
Why are my products still pinned when they go out of stock?
When pinning items using the Visual Editor, the option “Pinned items must match active filters to be displayed” ensures that only products matching the query filters are pinned.
If you wish for products to be automatically excluded from being pinned when they go out of stock,  follow these steps (as outlined in this guide article):

Have an attribute such as in_stock:true | false

Add a filter at the global level (either using a conditionless rule or by passing it for each query) to display only products with in_stock:true

When pinning a product, you make sure to keep this checkbox ticked.

This way, if a pinned product goes out of stock, it will be filtered out automatically. 
Please note that there is currently no option to keep out of stock products in your search results but have them automatically unpinned when they go out of stock. To bury (rather than hide) out-of-stock items, you would typically use Optional Filters, but these are overridden by pinning. 
This is because the engine applies the relevance strategies in the following order

Textual ranking criteria
Business relevance (custom ranking)

Personalisation (if you’ve enabled it and the engine has enough information to personalise results for a particular user)

Boosting and burying categories with Rules (optional filters)

Dynamic Re-Ranking

Pinning and hiding specific items with Rules


Since pinning is applied last, it takes priority and will override any previous burying logic, including Optional Filters.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/33835440585361-Why-are-my-products-still-pinned-when-they-go-out-of-stock

answer.answer:
Personalization re-ranks search results by adjusting the order to show items an individual user is most likely to engage with, based on their past behavior. This creates a more relevant, individualized experience for each person.

Here’s a breakdown of how it works and its effect on the result order.

### How Personalization Works

The core of personalization is the **user profile**, which is built from the events you send to Algolia.

1.  **Event Tracking**: To build a profile, you must send user events (like clicks, conversions, or adding to a cart) that are linked to a specific `userToken` [Why isn't personalization working?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7733358306705-The-debugger-shows-userTokens-in-my-events-why-isn-t-personalization-working).
2.  **Profile Building**: Algolia uses these events to create an affinity profile for each user, identifying their preferences for things like specific brands, categories, or authors. A profile can be started with just a single event, but the more events a user generates, the more accurate and impactful their personalization will be [Is there a minimum number of events required for Personalization?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/14533056574993-Is-there-a-minimum-number-of-events-required-for-Personalization).
3.  **Re-Ranking at Search Time**: When that same user searches, their `userToken` retrieves their profile. The personalization model then re-ranks the results returned by the core relevance algorithm, boosting items that match the user's affinities.

### Its Place in the Ranking Strategy

Personalization is one of several steps Algolia uses to determine the final order of results. The engine applies relevance strategies in a specific sequence:

1.  Textual ranking criteria and custom ranking
2.  **Personalization**
3.  Rules (boosting/burying categories)
4.  Dynamic Re-Ranking
5.  Rules (pinning/hiding specific items)

As you can see, personalization is applied after the initial relevance calculation but before other business-logic features like Dynamic Re-Ranking or pinned items via Rules [Why are my products still pinned when they go out of stock?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/33835440585361-Why-are-my-products-still-pinned-when-they-go-out-of-stock).

By default, if a user has a personalization profile, it will be used; if not (for example, for a new visitor), features like Dynamic Re-Ranking can be used as a fallback [How do Dynamic Re-Ranking and Personalization work together?](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38735971610385-How-do-Dynamic-Re-Ranking-and-Personalization-work-together).

Now that you know how personalization re-orders results, are you interested in how to measure its impact on your key metrics?

sources.text:
How do Dynamic Re-Ranking and Personalization work together?
By default, when both Personalization and Dynamic Re-Ranking (DRR) are enabled, the engine does not apply them concurrently. Instead, it applies:

Personalization for users with enough data to personalize the search

DRR for users without enough data to personalize the search (such as first-time users).
This means if the userToken sent with the request has an existing profile with affinities that match at least one hit on the first page of results, Personalization will take precedence, otherwise, DRR will be applied.You can find our full documentation on how Personalization fits into Algolia's relevancy strategy here.How does this impact results?While the above can mean users see personalized results over "popular" products, its important to take into consideration that personalization only takes into account the past 90 days of users' activity meaning only recent user interactions are considered.If there has been no activity within the 90 day period, or if products returned do not match a user's profile, results will be re-ranked by DRR instead.We've found that users prefer to see personalized results based on their previous search or buying patterns when possible, rather than simply "popular" items.Personalization with DRRIf you wish to enable Dynamic Re-Ranking even when Personalization affects the first page of results, set allowReRankingOnPersonalization to true at query time. This will mean:
Dynamic Re-Ranking pins promoted hits first
Personalization reorders the remaining hits (the unpinned hits) 
If Dynamic Re-Ranking promotes 100 hits, Personalization can only reorder hits starting at position 101.Note that if distinct > 1, DRR is not applied, even when allowReRankingOnPersonalization=true. To apply DRR, set distinct to 0 or 1.How to test their relative impact If you wish to test the impact of personalization vs DRR, you could run an A/B test where:
DRR and personalization is enabled on both indices
One variant has a personalizationImpact of 0 (to mimic enablePersonalization:false)
Set the other variant with personalizationImpact greater than 0
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/38735971610385-How-do-Dynamic-Re-Ranking-and-Personalization-work-together

sources.text:
Is there a minimum number of events required for Personalization?
Personalization works based on user profiles. We can start building a profile with a single event, there is not a fixed minimum number of events. 
Depending on the strategy and traffic it may require more than one event for a user to have a profile. In general, the more events, the more impact your Personalization strategy will have on the Search results.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/14533056574993-Is-there-a-minimum-number-of-events-required-for-Personalization

sources.text:
The debugger shows userTokens in my events, why isn't personalization working?
The events debugger logs events, not queries. You therefore need to ensure you are sending a userToken with each query, i.e. whenever a user searches so Algolia can match the users' events with their search queries.For Personalization to work effectively, the same userToken must be sent with both search requests and events. This is because:
The userToken sent with events is used to build user profiles.
The userToken sent with search queries is used to retrieve and apply those profiles at query time.
You can inspect your queries in the API Monitoring section pf your dashboard:
Go to the Algolia dashboard and select your Algolia application.
On the left sidebar, select API Monitoring

Open the Search API Logs page: https://www.algolia.com/apps/YOUR_APP_ID_HERE/monitoring/logs

Here, you can monitor requests and operations as they occur on Algolia’s servers. If a userToken is sent using the userToken parameter, it will appear in the request body. Alternatively, if it’s sent using the x-algolia-usertoken request header, it will appear in the request headers.For a full list of common userToken issues, troubleshooting topics and best practices, see our summary article here.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7733358306705-The-debugger-shows-userTokens-in-my-events-why-isn-t-personalization-working

questions.prompt: How do I configure searchable attributes the right way?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How do I configure searchable attributes the right way?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

To configure searchable attributes correctly, you should explicitly define them in your index configuration. This ensures consistent and predictable search behavior by giving you control over which attributes are searched and in what order of importance ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes)).

### Why Explicit Configuration is Important

By default, Algolia makes all attributes in your records searchable. However, this default behavior only works reliably if all your records share the exact same structure or schema. If some records are missing certain attributes, Algolia may not search them consistently, leading to unpredictable results.

Explicitly defining your **searchable attributes** is the recommended solution to avoid this. It allows you to:
*   **Ensure consistent search behavior** across all queries.
*   **Control which fields are searchable** and exclude those that aren't relevant.
*   **Prioritize more important attributes** to improve the relevance of your search results.

### Best Practices for Configuration

Here are some best practices to follow when setting up your searchable attributes.

#### Prioritize Your Attributes

The order in which you list your searchable attributes matters. Attributes listed higher up are considered more important by the ranking algorithm. For example, a match in a `title` attribute should usually rank higher than a match in a `description`.

*   **Ordered attributes**: By default, the position of a match within an attribute affects ranking. This is ideal for attributes like titles or brand names where a match at the beginning is more relevant.
*   **Unordered attributes**: You can declare an attribute as `unordered` if the position of a match within it shouldn't affect relevance. This is useful for attributes like tags or categories.

You can learn more about optimizing the order and types of attributes in this guide on [Configuring Searchable Attributes](https://academy.algolia.com/training/019465d6-0539-7661-8640-9b0c3656c165).

#### Structure Data for Large Text Fields

For records with very long text fields, like articles or detailed descriptions, it's a good practice to split the content into smaller records or chunks. Algolia only considers the positions of the first 1,000 words for exact phrase matching. Breaking up large content ensures that all parts of your text can be matched effectively ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4420156558481-Why-does-an-exact-phrase-query-fail-even-though-the-phrase-exists-in-my-record)).

### Configuration Tips

*   **Adding attributes in the dashboard**: If an attribute doesn't appear in the dropdown list in your Algolia dashboard, you can still add it by typing its name manually. The list is populated from a sample of your records, so it may not always be complete ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8014088474513-Why-don-t-all-of-my-attributes-appear-in-the-configuration-drop-down-inputs-on-the-Algolia-Dashboard)).
*   **Restricting attributes at query time**: For advanced use cases, you can use the **restrictSearchableAttributes** parameter to limit which attributes are searched for a specific query, rather than changing the overall index configuration ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/7642016208529-How-can-I-limit-the-attributes-that-are-searchable-at-query-time)).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Do synonyms work for facet value searches?
When you declare your attributes for faceting as "searchable", you can search for values within a given facet attribute.At this time however, synonyms don’t apply to facet value searches. In other words, a synonym configured in your index will apply to standard search queries, but it won’t be used when searching within a searchable facet.For a full list of common synonym issues, troubleshooting topics and best practices, see our summary article here.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/9525323477265-Do-synonyms-work-for-facet-value-searches

sources.text:
Why isn't Algolia searching all my records' attributes?
If Algolia doesn’t appear to be searching across every attribute in your records, the issue may be related to your Searchable Attributes configuration. This article explains how Algolia determines which attributes are searchable, and why explicitly configuring them can help ensure consistent behavior.Default behavior: all attributes are searchableBy default, when you create an index in Algolia and don't configure Searchable Attributes, every attribute in every record is searchable. This means you can start using search right away without any manual setup.So why might it not work as expected?If your records don’t all share the same schema, for example, if some objects are missing certain fields, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can lead to inconsistent search behavior. Algolia may not reliably search an attribute that isn't present in every record, making it seem like some attributes are being ignored.Recommended solution: define your searchable attributesTo avoid unpredictability, especially when your data structure varies between records, we strongly recommend configuring the Searchable Attributes explicitly in the Configuration tab of the Dashboard (or via the API). This lets you:
Control which fields are searchable
Prioritize more important attributes
Ensure consistent behavior across all queries
Summary
All attributes are searchable by default, but only when all records share the same schema.
If your records vary, leaving Searchable Attributes empty can cause unexpected results.
To ensure consistent and predictable search behavior, explicitly define your Searchable Attributes.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/37905596422545-Why-isn-t-Algolia-searching-all-my-records-attributes

sources.text:
Why does an exact phrase query fail even though the phrase exists in my record?
If an exact phrase appears in your data but doesn’t match in search, one possible cause is the position of that phrase within the attribute.Exact phrase matching depends on word positions. Algolia only keeps the positions of the first 1,000 words of each attribute. If the phrase appears beyond that point, the engine may not be able to match it as an exact phrase, even when the text is present in the record.This usually affects large text fields such as long descriptions, article bodies, or page content.To reduce the impact of this limitation, the best approach is to index smaller records so the searchable content is split into shorter sections. For long documents or pages, this often means breaking the content into chunks instead of storing everything in one large attribute.It can also help to review your searchableAttributes configuration so the most important content is prioritized in search. This won’t remove the 1,000-word position limit for phrase matching, but it can improve how relevant content is ranked and matched overall.If your use case depends heavily on exact phrase queries, check whether the phrase is located deep inside a long attribute. If it is, restructuring the indexed content into smaller records is usually the most reliable solution.Resources
Tokenization
Index long pages
searchableAttributes
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/4420156558481-Why-does-an-exact-phrase-query-fail-even-though-the-phrase-exists-in-my-record

sources.text:
Why don’t all of my attributes appear in the configuration drop down inputs on the Algolia Dashboard?
If you're not seeing certain attributes available to select from the dropdown menus of Attributes for faceting, Searchable attributes, Custom Ranking, or similar configuration sections in the Algolia Dashboard, it’s likely due to how the attribute list is generated.How attribute drop down inputs are populatedThe attributes available for selection are based on a "records sample" taken from your index. Specifically, this sample is built from the first 10 records returned by an empty search (i.e. a search with no query). If an attribute doesn't appear in any of those 10 records, it won’t show up in the dropdown list, even if it exists in other records.Adding attributes that don't appearYou can still add any attribute manually, even if it’s not listed:
Click into the dropdown.
Type the attribute name exactly as it appears in your records.
Select the option to add it manually (usually the first item in the autocomplete).
Warning messagesWhen adding an attribute manually, you may see a warning like the one below, stating that the attribute isn't present in all records. This is a safeguard to help prevent typos, but as long as the attribute name is correct, the configuration will still work as expected.This flexibility allows you to configure indexing behavior even for attributes that aren’t widely distributed across your dataset.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/8014088474513-Why-don-t-all-of-my-attributes-appear-in-the-configuration-drop-down-inputs-on-the-Algolia-Dashboard

sources.text:
[Shopify] How can I make the "named_tags" and/or "named_tags_names" attribute values searchable?
When you add a tag (ie: rainbow), it will be indexed in the tags attribute. 
 
When you add a key:value tags (ie: color: rainbow),  it will be indexed in the named_tags attribute, and the named_tags_names attribute will hold the property names (in this case, color). 
 
If you'd like the named_tags and/or named_tags_names values to be searchable, you can add these attributes as searchableAttributes in your Algolia index configuration.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17809003658641--Shopify-How-can-I-make-the-named-tags-and-or-named-tags-names-attribute-values-searchable

Please check that list to ensure that all preferred attributes are included. Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, and Personalization could be appliedBeyond applying the relevance criteria while ranking results, other factors could be at play due to an index's configuration. If you're using Rules, Dynamic Re-Ranking, or Personalization, then your search results' ranking could be affected. You can find out more about how these features are applied to ranking here: Effect on Relevance. While we can't comment on how other search engines and extensions rank their results, we hope this article gives you information regarding how Algolia ranks your search results.

answer.answer:
Configuring searchable attributes correctly is one of the most important steps to ensure relevant search results. It involves creating a prioritized list of the attributes in your records that you want Algolia to search in.

### How to Configure Searchable Attributes

The key is to list the attributes in order of importance. A match found in an attribute at the top of your list is considered more relevant than a match found in an attribute at the bottom. This is a foundational step for setting up your relevance.

Here are the best practices to follow, as detailed in [Configure searchable attributes the right way](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/managing-results/must-do/searchable-attributes/how-to/configuring-searchable-attributes-the-right-way):

1.  **Prioritize by Importance**: Start with attributes that contain the most specific and important information and move towards more general, descriptive data. A good order might be:
    *   Product name or article title
    *   Brand, artist, or author
    *   Categories
    *   SKU or reference numbers (if users search for them)
    *   General description

2.  **Use `ordered` and `unordered` Modifiers**: You can specify how Algolia should treat matches within an attribute.
    *   **`ordered` (default)**: Use this for attributes where the word position matters, like a product title. A match at the beginning of the attribute is treated as more important than a match at the end.
    *   **`unordered`**: Use this for attributes where word position is not important, such as tags, keywords, or categories. "blue" and "shirt" is the same as "shirt" and "blue".

3.  **Be Selective**: Only include attributes that contain useful, human-readable text. You should generally exclude:
    *   Numeric values that aren't used for searching (like database IDs)
    *   True/false values
    *   Image URLs

By carefully selecting and ordering your searchable attributes, you gain significant control over your search's relevance.

Now that your searchable attributes are set up, are you interested in how to further refine the order of results using custom ranking?

questions.prompt: How do I implement backend search with React InstantSearch?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How do I implement backend search with React InstantSearch?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

To implement a backend search with React InstantSearch, you need to create a backend endpoint that generates a **secured API key** for your frontend application. While many features of React InstantSearch can be used without a backend, implementing one is crucial for security and controlling access to your data ([details and examples](https'://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/react/')).

### Why Use a Backend?

The primary reason for a backend is to avoid leaking your Admin API Key and to control what data users can access. Your backend can generate a **secured API key** that grants limited permissions, such as restricting searches to a specific index or applying filters based on the user.

### How to Implement Backend Search

Implementing backend search involves creating an endpoint that your frontend can call to retrieve a secured API key. This key is then used to initialize the `searchClient` in your React InstantSearch application.

Here are the main steps:

1.  **Create a Backend Endpoint**: Set up an endpoint on your server (e.g., using Express for Node.js). This endpoint will be responsible for generating the secured API key.
2.  **Generate a Secured API Key**: In your backend, use an Algolia API client for your server's language. The client has a `generateSecuredApiKey` method that creates a temporary, restricted key. You can add parameters to this key, such as filters, to limit what the user can search for ([implementation guide](https'://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/in-depth/backend-instantsearch/react/')).
3.  **Fetch the API Key from the Frontend**: Your React application will make a request to your backend endpoint to get the secured API key.
4.  **Initialize the Search Client**: Use the fetched key to initialize the Algolia `searchClient` in your React InstantSearch implementation. This client, now using the secured key, will handle all search requests.

This setup ensures that your main API keys are never exposed on the client-side and that you have full control over search permissions.

> For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Senior Full-Stack Software Engineer, Frontend Experiences

At Algolia, we’re proud to be a pioneer and market leader in AI Search, empowering 17,000+ businesses to deliver blazing-fast, predictive search and browse experiences at internet scale. Every week, we power over 30 billion search requests — four times more than Microsoft Bing, Yahoo, Baidu, Yandex, and DuckDuckGo combined.

In 2021, we raised $150 million in Series D funding, quadrupling our valuation to $2.25 billion. This strong foundation enables us to keep investing in our market-leading platform and serving incredible customers like Under Armour, PetSmart, Stripe, Gymshark, and Walgreens.

The Team

The Frontend Experiences (FX) team is responsible for inspiring and empowering Algolia users to build the best Algolia powered UI experiences for their needs. We imagine, create and decompose elaborated UI experiences into small blocks ready to be mixed and matched in our users’ apps. We maintain a set of UI libraries such as Autocomplete, which you may have come across while searching the Tailwind or React docs. We also maintain the InstantSearch library platform which unlocks complex and modern Search & Discovery use cases.

We don’t use a single framework, we use them all. Our libraries work on top of Vue.js, React but also plain JavaScript (“Vanilla JS”). We write specs for every feature we release. Our code is in TypeScript, fully open source and tested. 

We don’t only do frontend, we also maintain certain backend features that support our experiences, in TypeScript, Go and Python.

The Opportunity

We're looking for a Fullstack Engineer to join the Frontend Experiences (FX) team at Algolia. As a member, you will have a direct impact on the look and feel of Algolia powered UIs. You’ll help customers re-imagine the future of how people search and discover information. You will play a pivotal role in architecting our platform, which is the face of Algolia’s Developer Experience.

Your role will consist of: 

  • Architect, design and develop new versions of InstantSearch platform, including our open source libraries and our backend APIs.
  • Design and implement features that leverage agentic patterns within the Algolia platform and our open-source libraries.
  • Implement new features, solve issues and analyse user's feedback on our popular open-source projects
  • Coordinate with our product, design and marketing teams on the release of new projects and websites
  • Take ownership, research, explore and deliver novel experiences
  • Teach us what we don't know and how you want to improve the search and discovery experiences
  • Participate (new features, bug fixes) to other popular open-source projects related to our project needs
  • Be a technical mentor and help the team grow
  • Help define engineering best practices and processes

You might be a fit if you have:

  • Demonstrable proficiency with the latest agentic experience design, development patterns, and relevant SDKs.
  • Deep understanding of building, integrating, and optimizing user interfaces leveraging autonomous agents, LLMs, and AI-driven workflows.
  • Good knowledge of JavaScript, TypeScript, the Frontend web ecosystem: DOM, frameworks, library crafting, bundlers
  • You have created at least one reusable module (private or open source)
  • You have an eye for great user experiences (UX)
  • You like to help, teach and mentor your colleagues, in and outside the team.
  • Experience at our current stage and beyond ($50-300M ARR range, high growth, lots of change and building internal infrastructure).

Overall, we care about your current and future skill set. Not where you studied or who you know. We care about what you like to do and about what you'd like to do in the future, at Algolia.



#LI-Remote

FLEXIBLE WORKPLACE STRATEGY:

Algolia’s flexible workplace model is designed to empower all Algolians to fulfill our mission to power search and discovery with ease. We place an emphasis on an individual’s impact, contribution, and output, over their physical location. Algolia is a high-trust environment and many of our team members have the autonomy to choose where they want to work and when. 

We have a global presence with offices in Paris, NYC, London, Sydney and Bucharest, however we also offer many of our team members the option to work remotely either as fully remote or hybrid-remote employees. Positions listed as "Remote" are only available for remote work within the specified country. Positions listed within a specific city are only available in that location - depending on the role it may be available with either a hybrid-remote or in-office schedule.

WE’RE LOOKING FOR SOMEONE WHO CAN LIVE OUR VALUES:

  • GRIT - Problem-solving and perseverance capability in an ever-changing and growing environment.
  • TRUST - Willingness to trust our co-workers and to take ownership.
  • CANDOR - Ability to receive and give constructive feedback.
  • CARE - Genuine care about other team members, our clients and the decisions we make in the company.
  • HUMILITY - Aptitude for learning from others, putting ego aside.

We’re looking for talented, passionate people to help build the world’s best search and discovery technology. We value autonomy, diversity, and collaboration. We’re committed to creating an inclusive workplace where everyone is respected and supported—regardless of race, age, ancestry, religion, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, marital status, color, veteran status, disability, or socioeconomic background.

IMPORTANT NOTICE FOR CANDIDATES - Recruitment Fraud Notice

We’ve recently seen an increase in recruitment scams targeting job seekers. To help protect yourself, please keep the following in mind:

  • Our open positions may appear on third-party job boards, but the best way to apply safely is directly through our careers page.
  • All genuine communication from Algolia will come from an @algolia.com email address. If you receive an email from someone claiming to work at Algolia who does not have an @algolia.com email address, please do not respond or share any personal information.
  • We’ll never ask for payments, purchases, or financial details during the hiring process.

READY TO APPLY?

If you share our values and our enthusiasm for building the world’s best search & discovery technology, we’d love to review your application!

answer.answer:
Of course. To implement backend search with React InstantSearch, you create a custom `searchClient` in your frontend that sends search requests to your own server. Your server then securely calls the Algolia API and returns the results.

This approach keeps your Algolia API key private and allows you to add custom logic on your backend before or after the search query.

### How it works

The implementation involves two main parts: a custom search client on your frontend and an API endpoint on your backend.

#### 1. Frontend: The custom search client

Instead of using the standard Algolia search client in your React app, you provide a custom one to your `<InstantSearch>` component. This client doesn't call Algolia directly. Instead, it calls your backend endpoint, sending the search requests for processing.

The core of this client is a `search` method that forwards requests from multiple Algolia indices to your server, as detailed in the guide to [Backend search with React InstantSearch](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/react/).

#### 2. Backend: The API endpoint

Your backend needs an API endpoint (for example, `/api/search`) that receives these requests from your frontend client. In this endpoint, you'll use an official Algolia API client (like the one for Node.js, Python, or PHP) to perform the actual search. This is where you use your secret Algolia API key, ensuring it's never exposed to the browser.

Once you receive the results from Algolia, your endpoint sends them back to the frontend. The [implementation guide](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/going-further/backend-search/in-depth/backend-instantsearch/react/) provides more detail on this server-side setup.

Are you implementing this for security reasons to protect your API key, or are you also planning to use it for server-side rendering (SSR)?

questions.prompt: How do I send click and conversion events to Algolia Insights?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "How do I send click and conversion events to Algolia Insights?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

You can send click and conversion events to Algolia Insights using various methods, including front-end libraries like Autocomplete and InstantSearch, integrations such as Google Tag Manager, or directly via the Insights API. It is crucial to include key parameters like the `userToken` and `queryID` with each event.

### Implementation Methods

There are several ways to send events to the Algolia Insights API depending on your setup.

*   **Autocomplete Plugin**: For users of the Autocomplete library, you can use the `createAlgoliaInsightsPlugin` to automatically send click and conversion events ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/ui-libraries/autocomplete/api-reference/autocomplete-plugin-algolia-insights/createAlgoliaInsightsPlugin/)).
*   **InstantSearch Libraries**: When using libraries like Vue InstantSearch, you can set the `insights` option to `true` to enable the sending of click and conversion events ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/building-search-ui/installation/vue/)).
*   **Google Tag Manager (GTM)**: You can configure GTM to send click, conversion, and view events to the Insights API ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/sending-events/connectors/google-tag-manager/)).
*   **BigCommerce Integration**: If you are using BigCommerce, you can configure the integration to send click and conversion events automatically ([details and examples](https://www.algolia.com/doc/integration/bigcommerce/search-settings/events/)).

### Key Parameters for Events

For events to be processed correctly and provide meaningful analytics, you must include specific parameters.

*   **`userToken`**: A unique identifier for each user. This is essential for the analytics dashboard to record individual user actions and is required for the Insights API to accept the event ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/14010751652625-Why-do-I-see-Warning-Number-of-users-is-low-compared-to-volume-of-searches-in-my-analytics-dashboard-)).
*   **`queryID`**: This ID connects a search request to a subsequent click or conversion event. It's crucial for features like Dynamic Re-Ranking and for accurate analytics. Events sent without a `queryID` can cause issues with these features.
*   **`index`**: The name of the index where the search occurred must be declared for the event to be valid.
*   **`objectIDs` and `positions`**: For click events, you must include the `objectID` of the clicked item and its `position` in the search results.

### Debugging Common Errors

If your events are not being processed, you may receive a `422` error code. You can use the Events Debugger in your Algolia dashboard to identify issues. Common errors include:
*   Missing `userToken`, `index`, or `objectIDs`.
*   An incorrect number of `objectIDs` and `positions` for a click event.
*   Invalid syntax for the `eventName`.
*   Conversion events missing the required `eventSubtype` (e.g., `addToCart` or `purchase`).

A detailed guide on debugging these errors is available to help you troubleshoot ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27738943151249-Why-am-I-receiving-a-422-error-How-to-debug-your-events)).

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
Why do I see "Warning: Number of users is low compared to volume of searches" in my analytics dashboard?
The userToken needs to be sent with the query at query time in order for the analytics dashboard to record all of your individual users.
When sending click and conversion events to Algolia, you will also need to send the userToken with each event in order for it to be accepted by the insights API.
The warning is there to highlight that you need to look into how your events are implemented.

sources.text:
How can I add more events in Magento without using Personalization
If you do not want to use Personalization, but you do want to capture multiple events, ensure that click and conversion analytics are turned on. If they are, then you can still obtain data on your users behaviour. 
We recommend reading this general guide on planning which events to send.  
You can add additional events using code to customize your front end. See our guide here on the topic of adding new events by registering the custom hook afterInsightsBindEvents(algoliaInsights).

sources.text:
Why am I receiving a 422 error? How to debug your events
If your event requests are failing, you can use the Events Debugger to find out why. The most common error code is 422, and you can find these in the debugger in either of the following ways:

Type httpCode=422 in the search box to show only the events with the HTTP status code 422 
Select Event Validity > False in the Filter Events button to show all events where errors=true


We can find a detailed guide on investigating if your click & conversion events are valid here.
Below you can find a list of the most commonly seen errors and how to fix them:
 
The index field is required
An index must be declared in order for events to be valid. This must be same as the index name used by the search engine.
 
Cannot process more that 20 objects in an event
You can include up to 20 objectIDs in a single event. You can read more on this here.
 
Cannot process more than 10 filters in an event
You can include up to 20 filters in a single event. You can read more on this here.
 
Missing userToken parameter
User tokens are strings that uniquely identify users throughout your app. The Search Insights library sets a default anonymous userToken. You can also manually set the userToken or authenticatedUserToken parameter in two ways: either globally or for each event. You can read more about sending userTokens here.
 
ObjectIDs[0] is required
ObjectID field is missing objectIDs. This cannot be an empty string or null value. If sending events unrelated to an Algolia request, we recommend setting an objectID at index time and passing this value.
 
Event of type click should have the same number of ObjectIDs and Positions
When sending click events with a queryID, you must pass an array of positions as the value for the positions parameter with one position per objectID. Algolia uses the positions parameter to calculate the average click position. We have another guide article on this here.
 
Events should specify either some objectIDs or some Filters
Events are not being processed due to events not specifying objectIDs or filters. In order for conversion events to be valid, they must contain either an objectIDs OR a filters parameter. A single event must not include both objectIDs and filters. 
 
Only event of type click should specify the positions attribute
The positions parameter is only required for click events with a queryID and not view or conversion events.
 
Invalid eventName syntax
Event names need to be between 1 and 64 characters long and contain only visible ascii characters. You can read more on the eventName parameter here.
 
Invalid type for field [parameter]: expected string, got number
The specified field holds the wrong value type, for example, it holds an integer while it should hold a string value. A list of the value types each parameter should hold can be found here.
 
QueryID must be a search queryID
Algolia uses the queryID to connect a search or browse request to a click or conversion event and therefore the queryID sent with an event must match that generated for the corresponding search/browse request. You can read more on sending queryIDs here.
 
Conversion event must include eventSubtype
Conversion events must include an eventSubtype parameter,  You will need to ensure you are sending valid subtypes for all conversion events - either addToCart or purchase. For all other events it should be omitted.
 
The timestamp must be at most 4 days in the past
The timestamp must be at most 4 days in the past - if you’re doing backend search, it’s important to set correct timestamps.
 
UserToken must contain only alphanumeric, equal, plus, slash, hyphen or underscore characters
User tokens are strings that uniquely identify users throughout your app. You can read more about them here.
 
Price must be a valid decimal number and total length must be no longer than 16 characters
Price must be a decimal number with no more than 16 characters (including non-digit characters).
 
Event of type click with positions should specify a queryID
When sending click events with positions, you must also send a queryID.
 
Event of type click may only have strictly positive positions
Positions values must start with a 1 and not 0. The first search result has a position of 1 (not 0). The position is absolute, and not relative, to any results page. For example, if there are 10 results per page, the position of the third record of the second results page is 13. Since page starts at 0, you can use the following formula:$positionOnPage + $page * hitsPerPage.
 
Invalid Payload
If you are getting an "invalid payload" error when sending events to the Insights API, we would recommend reviewing your event data parameters to make sure they are in the expected format. We have another guide article on this here. 
 
Events are being sent without a queryID. 
While these instances do not raise errors, it will create issues should you wish to use features such as Dynamic Re-Ranking or Analytics as Algolia uses the queryID to connect a search or browse request to a click or conversion event. You can find more info on keeping track of queryIDs here.
sources.label: https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/27738943151249-Why-am-I-receiving-a-422-error-How-to-debug-your-events

answer.answer:
You can send click and conversion events to Algolia using front-end libraries like Autocomplete or InstantSearch, or directly through the Insights API clients for various programming languages. These events are essential for powering features like A/B Testing, Personalization, and AI Re-ranking.

### How to Send Events

There are a few primary methods for sending events, depending on your implementation.

#### Using UI Libraries (Simplest method)
If you're using Algolia's UI libraries, they can handle sending events automatically.
*   **Autocomplete**: The [Algolia Insights plugin for Autocomplete](https://www.algolia.com/doc/ui-libraries/autocomplete/api-reference/autocomplete-plugin-algolia-insights/) automatically sends events when a user interacts with the autocomplete menu.
*   **InstantSearch**: InstantSearch widgets have a built-in option, `sendEvent`, that lets you send click and conversion events on user interactions.

#### Using Connectors
You can use connectors like [Google Tag Manager](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/sending-events/connectors/google-tag-manager/) to send click, conversion, and view events to the Insights API without extensive custom coding.

#### Using the Insights API Clients
For more control or for backend implementations, you can use the [Insights API clients](https://www.algolia.com/doc/guides/sending-events/send-events-with-api-clients/). This involves adding a small amount of code to your application.

Here's an example of sending a click event using the JavaScript client:

```javascript
// After a search
aa('clickedObjectIDsAfterSearch', {
  index: 'your_index_name',
  eventName: 'Product Clicked',
  queryID: 'the_query_id', // The queryID from the search response
  objectIDs: ['objectID1', 'objectID2'], // The objectIDs of the clicked items
  positions: [7, 8], // The position of the clicked items in the search results
  userToken: 'user-12345', // A unique identifier for the user
});
```

### Key Event Details

When sending events, you need to include several key pieces of information:
*   **`userToken`**: An anonymous identifier for the user to enable personalization.
*   **`index`**: The name of the index where the search occurred.
*   **`eventName`**: A descriptive name for the event (e.g., "Product Added to Cart").
*   **`queryID`**: The unique ID generated by a search query. This is crucial for linking an event back to a specific search. You must set `clickAnalytics: true` in your search query to receive this ID.
*   **`objectIDs` or `filters`**: The specific products or content the user interacted with, identified by their `objectID` or by the `filters` used (e.g., for a category conversion).

Are you currently using a front-end library like InstantSearch, or are you planning to implement this on your backend?

questions.prompt: What is NeuralSearch and how does it rank results?

answer.answer: [WEBSITE STUB] No live algolia.com Ask-AI capture wired yet for: "What is NeuralSearch and how does it rank results?". This placeholder exists so the harness runs end-to-end; lab/capture will replace it.

Algolia **NeuralSearch** is an AI-powered search technology that enhances traditional keyword search by understanding the underlying concepts and intent behind a user's query. It ranks results using a combined "neural score" that evaluates both keyword relevance and semantic, or conceptual, similarity.

### What is NeuralSearch?

NeuralSearch is an AI model that learns from your user engagement signals, such as clicks and conversions, to understand the relationships between words and concepts in your specific domain. Instead of only matching the exact keywords in a search query, it also returns results that match the *concept* of what the user is looking for ([details and examples](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results)). This allows it to surface more relevant results, even when there are no direct keyword matches.

### How NeuralSearch Ranks Results

NeuralSearch uses a hybrid scoring system to rank results, calculating a final **neural score** for each item. This score is a combination of two other scores: a keyword score and a semantic score ([details](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/articles/17700562878481-How-does-NeuralSearch-rank-results)).

#### Keyword Score
This score represents how well a record matches the query based on traditional textual relevance. It is calculated using Algolia’s standard **tie-breaking algorithm**, which considers factors like the number of matching words, proximity, and attribute priority. A record receives a keyword score if it is found through the standard keyword search process.

#### Semantic Score
The semantic score measures how well a record matches the *concept* of the query. This score is based on **vector search** and "neural hash similarity." It determines the conceptual relevance of a result even if the keywords don't match exactly. A record has a semantic score if it is found through vector search.

#### Neural Score
The final **neural score** combines the keyword and semantic scores to produce the final ranking. This score measures how relevant a record is compared to all other records in the result set for that specific query.

For more support or info, please feel free to browse our [support articles](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/search) or [docs](https://www.algolia.com/doc). If you still need help, [submit a ticket](https://support.algolia.com/hc/en-us/requests/new) for customer support.

sources.text:
How does NeuralSearch rank results?
Search with NeuralSearchSearching with NeuralSearch works the same as classic search, except with an increased understanding of the query. Now, instead of matching only keywords in the text, the search engine also returns results matching the concept of what the searcher is looking for.NeuralSearch is an association based AI model that is trained by your own click and conversion events, and uses vectors in order to rank results.How NeuralSearch ranks resultsNeuralSearch results have a neural score composed of a keyword score, a semantic score, or both.Due to the number of criteria used to rank each specific result, it's not possible to definitively describe why a result is ranked on a specific position.Keyword score
The keyword score represents how well a record matches the query from a keyword search against other keyword-retrieved results. At the base of this score is Algolia’s tie-breaking algorithm. A record has a keyword score if keyword search finds it.Semantic score
The semantic score represents how well a record matches the query from a vector search against other vector-retrieved results. At the basis of this score is neural hash similarity. A record has a semantic score if vector search finds it.Neural score
The neural score combines the keyword score and the semantic score. It measures how relevant the record is compared to the other records in the result set. The neural score answers the question: “Against this query, compared to these other results, how relevant is this result?”You can find an extended explanation of NeuralSearch on this blog article.
